hints: Check if timelocks are anchored to the correct event (e.g., draw completion, not contract initialization)
Verify that timelocks are updated when the reference event occurs (e.g., redraw, new round)
Look for constant calculations involving time units -- verify they produce correct values
Identify admin-changeable parameters (fees, gas costs, thresholds) and check if they affect existing commitments
Verify that admin-changeable parameters have upper bounds or require a timelock for changes
Check if admin can prevent protocol functions (startDraw, fulfillRandomWords) from executing
preconditions:
- Contract has admin-controlled functions that can recover locked assets or modify critical parameters
- Timelock or cooldown mechanisms are improperly calculated or anchored to the wrong reference point
- Admin can prevent protocol functions from executing (e.g., not starting a draw, underfunding VRF)
- Dynamically changeable protocol parameters (fees, gas costs) apply retroactively to existing orders or positions

hints: Identify `payable` functions that accept ETH for order execution or purchases
Check if the function calculates a price dynamically (auction) and whether excess ETH is refunded
Verify that ETH withdrawal/rescue functions use `address(this).balance` rather than `msg.value`
Look for functions that support both ETH and ERC20 currencies -- check if `msg.value > 0` is rejected for ERC20 paths
Check if accumulated ETH fees in the contract are retrievable by the owner/protocol
preconditions:
- Contract accepts native ETH payments via `payable` functions for order execution
- The contract supports both ETH and ERC20 payment currencies using the same function
- Overpayment of ETH is not refunded to the caller
- ETH accumulated in the contract cannot be withdrawn due to a bug in the rescue/withdraw function
- Auction-style pricing (Dutch auction, reverse Dutch auction) makes exact payment amounts unpredictable at submission time

hints: Identify contracts inheriting ERC721 or ERC1155 that override transfer functions with custom checks
Count the number of public/external transfer functions in the base contract (ERC721 has 3: `transferFrom`, `safeTransferFrom(3 args)`, `safeTransferFrom(4 args)`)
Verify that ALL transfer variants are overridden with the same restriction logic
Check for ERC1155 contracts that override `safeTransferFrom` but not `safeBatchTransferFrom`
Consider using `_beforeTokenTransfer` hooks instead, which apply to all transfer paths
preconditions:
- Contract inherits from a token standard (ERC721, ERC1155) that exposes multiple transfer functions
- Custom transfer restrictions are implemented by overriding only a subset of transfer functions
- The base contract exposes additional transfer variants (e.g., `safeTransferFrom(address,address,uint256,bytes)` or `safeBatchTransferFrom`) that are not overridden
- Tokens have transfer-limiting properties such as staking locks, non-transferability flags, or game state dependencies

hints: Search for role-granting functions (`addMinter`, `addStaker`, `setAllowedBurningAddresses`, etc.)
Check if a corresponding revocation function exists for each granting function
If using OpenZeppelin AccessControl, verify that `DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE` is granted to an appropriate address in the constructor
Look for usage of the deprecated `_setupRole` instead of `_grantRole`
Check if permission mappings only ever set values to `true` with no mechanism to set them to `false`
preconditions:
- Contract uses a role-based access control system (custom or OpenZeppelin AccessControl)
- Functions exist to grant roles but no corresponding function to revoke them
- The `DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE` is not properly assigned, preventing governance of other roles
- The contract uses deprecated patterns like `_setupRole` instead of `_grantRole` with proper admin configuration

hints: Identify NFT exchange/marketplace contracts that handle multiple token standards
Check if `supportsInterface` calls determine which transfer method to use
Verify the order of interface checks -- ERC1155 should typically be checked before ERC721 for dual-standard tokens
Look for missing `revert` or `require` when no known interface is detected
Check if `numTokens` from ERC1155 orders is respected when falling back to ERC721 transfer paths
Identify tokens in the wild that support both interfaces (e.g., The Sandbox ASSET token)
preconditions:
- Contract handles transfers of NFTs that may implement ERC721, ERC1155, or both standards
- Interface detection via `supportsInterface` is used to select the transfer method
- The transfer function falls through silently (no revert) when neither interface is detected
- Some NFT collections implement both ERC721 and ERC1155 interfaces simultaneously

hints: Trace order matching flow end-to-end: validation -> matching -> execution -> transfer
Check if empty `tokenIds` arrays are handled -- they should either cause a revert or have explicit fill logic
Verify that `seller != buyer` is enforced in all matching paths
Ensure `numConstructedItems` (actual items being transferred) is validated against both buy and sell constraints
Look for `doItemsIntersect` returning `true` when one side specifies no tokens
Check if the Complication contract is consulted in ALL order execution paths (including `takeMultipleOneOrders`)
Verify that duplicate token IDs within the same collection are detected and rejected
preconditions:
- NFT marketplace or exchange contract matches buy orders with sell orders
- Order matching logic validates token counts, price, and intersection but has incomplete checks
- Off-chain matching engine submits matched orders to on-chain execution
- Orders support flexible specifications like "any token in collection" (empty tokenId arrays)
- The matching function does not verify that seller and buyer are distinct addresses

hints: Check if the VRF subscription is funded/controlled by the same entity that can trigger redraws
Look for redraw/re-request functions that can be called before the VRF response arrives
Verify that the minimum redraw cooldown exceeds the maximum VRF response time (24 hours for Chainlink V2)
Identify NFT minting functions where attribute determination and minting occur in the same transaction
Check if `_safeMint` is used (allows callback) combined with deterministic attribute generation
Look for on-chain randomness derived from `block.timestamp`, `msg.sender`, or other predictable values
preconditions:
- Contract uses Chainlink VRF or other oracle-based randomness for draw/raffle outcomes
- The draw organizer controls the VRF subscription funding or can trigger redraws
- Random numbers or attribute calculations use weak on-chain entropy (block properties, deterministic hashing)
- The randomness consumer allows redraw/re-request before the oracle response arrives
- Users can observe or predict the outcome within the same transaction and conditionally revert

category_title: Reentrancy in Reward and NFT Claiming
hints: Identify functions that mint/transfer ERC721 or ERC1155 tokens to `msg.sender` or arbitrary addresses
Check if state updates (nonces, claim counters, round trackers) occur **after** the external call
Look for `_safeMint`, `safeTransferFrom`, or `mintFromMergingPool` calls that trigger callback hooks
Verify whether a `nonReentrant` modifier or equivalent guard is present
Check if the function can be re-entered through a separate entry point (e.g., `takeOrders` re-entering `matchOneToManyOrders`)
preconditions:
- Contract mints or transfers ERC721/ERC1155 tokens during a claim or reward function
- State variables tracking claims (e.g., nonces, round counters) are updated **after** external calls
- The recipient address can be a contract (smart contract wallet) that implements `onERC721Received` or similar callback hooks
- No reentrancy guard (`nonReentrant` modifier) is applied to the claiming function

category_title: Staking Rounding and Dust Amount Exploits
hints: Look for staking factor calculations that use integer division with large denominators (`/ 10**18`)
Check if zero results from division are rounded up to 1 or otherwise given non-zero rewards
Verify if minimum stake amounts are enforced
Calculate the ratio of reward-per-stake for dust amounts versus normal amounts
For vault/pool patterns, check if share calculation is susceptible to inflation (large donation before first deposit)
Look for `sqrt` operations on small values that truncate to 0 then get bumped to 1
preconditions:
- Staking or reward calculations involve integer division that can round to zero
- A minimum stake amount is not enforced, allowing dust/wei-level stakes
- Rounding of zero results to one (floor-to-one pattern) gives disproportionate rewards for tiny stakes
- The protocol uses a staking factor or multiplier derived from the staked amount via square root or division
- Share-based vault or staking pool is susceptible to inflation attacks via direct token transfers

category_title: Unbounded Loop DoS in Reward Claims
hints: Identify reward/claim functions that loop from a stored checkpoint to the current round/epoch
Check if the loop has any upper bound or maximum iteration count per call
Look for nested loops inside the main iteration (e.g., iterating over winners per round)
Estimate gas cost per iteration -- if it involves SLOAD, external calls, or minting, even moderate round counts can hit the block gas limit
Check if the protocol can advance rounds without user participation, causing the gap to grow unboundedly
preconditions:
- Reward claiming function iterates over all unclaimed rounds/epochs since the user's last claim
- No upper bound or pagination mechanism limits the number of iterations per transaction
- The protocol operates in rounds/epochs and users may not claim for many rounds
- The gas cost per iteration is non-trivial (e.g., nested loops, storage reads, or external calls per round)

hints: Identify state variables that are set in setter functions rather than in the constructor
Check if functions using these variables validate they are non-zero or properly initialized
Look for default-zero values that would bypass time locks, duration checks, or modular arithmetic
Check for mappings that are partially initialized (e.g., only for generation 0 or round 0)
Verify that the deployment/initialization sequence is atomic or that the contract is paused until fully configured
Look for `% 0` operations that would cause a panic revert for uninitialized divisors
preconditions:
- Contract relies on state variables that must be set by an admin/owner after deployment
- Critical functions do not verify that these variables have been initialized before use
- Default zero values for uninitialized variables cause validation checks to be bypassed or arithmetic to fail
- There is a window between deployment and initialization where the contract is vulnerable

hints: Identify NFT minting/re-rolling functions that accept user parameters influencing attributes
Check if `fighterType`, `customAttributes`, or `dna` inputs are validated against expected ranges
Look for deterministic attribute generation where same inputs always produce same outputs
Verify that type-specific limits (e.g., `maxRerollsAllowed[fighterType]`) cannot be bypassed by supplying a different type
Check if mint pass redemption or claim functions allow users to freely specify token properties
preconditions:
- NFT minting or re-rolling functions accept user-controlled parameters that determine token attributes (element, weight, type, DNA, etc.)
- The function does not validate or constrain user-supplied inputs to expected ranges
- Attribute generation is deterministic based on user-supplied inputs, allowing attribute selection via input manipulation
- The generated attributes affect in-game mechanics (battle outcomes, rarity, value)

hints: Search for explicit type casts from larger to smaller integer types: `uint128(x)`, `uint64(x)`, `int256(x)` from `uint256` -- these silently truncate in Solidity
Look for subtraction operations where the second operand could be larger than the first, especially in accounting logic (balances, reserves, supply tracking)
Check `unchecked` blocks for arithmetic that could overflow or underflow
For Solidity < 0.8.0, verify that all arithmetic uses SafeMath or equivalent overflow-safe libraries
Look for multiplication chains where intermediate results can exceed `uint256` range before being divided
Check loop index variables: if a `uint` index counts something that could exceed its type's max, the loop never terminates
preconditions:
- The contract performs arithmetic on unsigned integers where subtraction can produce negative results or multiplication can exceed `type(uint256).max`
- Solidity version < 0.8.0 is used without SafeMath, or `unchecked` blocks are used in Solidity >= 0.8.0
- Values are cast from larger types to smaller types (e.g., `uint256` to `uint128`, `uint64`, or `int256`) without range validation
- Token amounts, timestamps, or computed values can reach extreme magnitudes under adversarial conditions

hints: Check all arithmetic involving token amounts for hardcoded decimal assumptions (e.g., dividing by `1e18` when the token might have 6 or 8 decimals)
Look for `preciseMul`, `preciseDiv`, or WAD-based math where inputs may not actually be in WAD precision
Verify that fixed-point math library functions (`expWad`, `lnWad`, `powWad`) receive inputs in their expected precision (typically 1e18)
Check for division operations where small numerators divided by large denominators can round to zero, allowing free tokens or zero-fee operations
Look for price calculations between token pairs with mismatched decimals -- the scaling factor must account for `18 - componentDecimals + quoteDecimals`
Search for `uint128` or smaller type casts from `uint256` values that could silently overflow
preconditions:
- The protocol interacts with tokens that have different decimal places (e.g., WETH at 18 decimals, USDC at 6 decimals, WBTC at 8 decimals)
- Mathematical operations assume a fixed decimal precision (e.g., 18 decimals / WAD) but receive values in a different precision
- Price or amount calculations involve multiplication or division where precision loss compounds
- Fixed-point math libraries (e.g., `expWad`, `lnWad`, `preciseMul`) are used with inputs outside their intended precision range

hints: Identify all `transferFrom` calls that move tokens into the contract
Check whether the contract records or uses the nominal `amount` parameter directly for accounting, rather than measuring the actual balance change via `balanceOf` before and after the transfer
Look for swap functions where the input amount from `transferFrom` is used in output calculations without verifying actual received amount
Check if minimum output checks (`require(amountOut >= minAmountOut)`) use the pre-fee amount, which would pass even when the user receives fewer tokens than expected
Verify whether the protocol documentation or token whitelisting logic explicitly excludes fee-on-transfer tokens
preconditions:
- The protocol interacts with ERC20 tokens that charge a fee on every `transfer()` or `transferFrom()` call (e.g., deflationary tokens, tax tokens)
- The contract assumes the amount received equals the amount specified in the transfer call
- Balance accounting relies on the transfer input amount rather than measuring the actual balance change
- The protocol does not whitelist tokens or explicitly exclude fee-on-transfer tokens

hints: Check if flash loan functions allow user-specified token addresses without validating them against known/whitelisted tokens
Look for health factor, collateral ratio, or solvency checks that can be satisfied temporarily using borrowed funds within a single transaction
Identify functions that transfer assets before verifying payment (implicit flash-swap vulnerability)
Check if flash loan fee calculations use the correct token and are collected from the right address
Verify that flash loan callbacks validate the return value matches the expected hash (`keccak256("ERC3156FlashBorrower.onFlashLoan")`)
Look for protocol fees on flash loans that should be distributed to the protocol treasury but are not
preconditions:
- The protocol offers flash loan functionality or interacts with systems that do (e.g., Aave, dYdX, Uniswap)
- On-chain state (prices, collateral ratios, health factors) can be temporarily manipulated within a single transaction
- The contract does not validate that economic conditions persist across multiple blocks
- Flash loan fee accounting, token address validation, or callback verification contains errors

hints: Identify functions where execution order matters: authorization/revocation, price updates, auction bids, liquidations, token swaps
Check if privileged operations (like `unauthorize`) can be circumvented by the target front-running with counter-actions (like `authorize` on an accomplice)
Look for oracle price setter functions without commit-reveal patterns -- attackers can see pending price updates and trade ahead
Check for Dutch auction mechanisms where a front-runner can purchase at a favorable price by seeing others' transactions
Verify if recovery/cancellation mechanisms can be grief-attacked by front-running with blocking transactions
Look for functions that read and act on state that can be modified in the same block by a front-runner
preconditions:
- Sensitive transactions are visible in the mempool before inclusion in a block (public Ethereum mainnet)
- The protocol has functions where the order of execution affects the outcome (e.g., auctions, authorization changes, price updates, recovery operations)
- No commit-reveal scheme, private mempool, or time-lock protection is implemented
- The attacker can submit transactions with higher gas prices to guarantee earlier execution

hints: Search for external/public functions that modify critical state variables (controller, owner, oracle, fee parameters) without access control modifiers
Check if `address(0)` is a valid caller for any access-controlled function due to missing zero-address validation on the privileged role
Look for OpenZeppelin's `Ownable` with `renounceOwnership` still accessible -- this can permanently brick admin functionality
Verify that initialization functions (in proxy/upgradeable patterns) can only be called once and by the deployer
Check if permissioned functions use `_msgSender()` (which respects meta-transactions) when they should use `msg.sender` directly
Look for functions that allow a privileged role to set arbitrary addresses that then gain elevated access (indirect privilege escalation)
preconditions:
- Critical state-changing functions lack `onlyOwner`, `onlyAdmin`, or role-based access modifiers
- Default values for privileged addresses are `address(0)`, and zero-address checks are missing
- Functions intended for trusted callers (governance, admin, keeper) are externally callable by anyone
- Proxy or upgradeable patterns leave initialization functions unprotected

hints: Search for swap, buy, sell, addLiquidity, and removeLiquidity functions that lack a `deadline` or `expiration` parameter
Check if AMM router calls (Uniswap, SushiSwap) pass `type(uint256).max` or `block.timestamp` as the deadline, which effectively disables the protection
Look for signed messages/orders that authorize financial operations without an expiration timestamp
Verify that all price-sensitive operations allow callers to specify a maximum acceptable execution time
Check if pending operations (credit loans, bids, offers) can be executed arbitrarily far in the future
preconditions:
- The protocol performs swaps, liquidity operations, or trades that interact with AMMs (Uniswap, SushiSwap, etc.)
- Functions that modify token balances based on price calculations do not accept or enforce a deadline parameter
- Transactions can remain pending in the mempool for extended periods (network congestion, low gas price)
- Price-sensitive operations execute at whatever the current price is when finally mined, not when submitted

hints: Check if the protocol uses AMM spot prices (e.g., `getReserves()`, `getAmountsOut()`) for any valuation -- these are trivially manipulable within a single transaction
For Chainlink feeds, verify that `latestRoundData()` return values are properly validated: check `updatedAt` for staleness, `answeredInRound >= roundId`, and `price > 0`
Look for fallback oracle mechanisms and verify they are actually reachable -- check if primary oracle reverts are caught with try/catch
Examine if low-TVL pools can be used as price sources, especially for Uniswap V3 positions where attackers can create their own pools with minimal liquidity
Verify that oracle price deviation bounds treat upward and downward movements symmetrically
Check if asset removal and re-addition preserves or resets oracle state (stale prices can persist)
preconditions:
- The protocol relies on an on-chain oracle for asset pricing (e.g., Uniswap TWAP, Chainlink, custom floor oracles)
- The oracle can return stale, manipulable, or incorrect prices under certain conditions
- Fallback oracle mechanisms are absent, misconfigured, or use spot prices vulnerable to flash loans
- Low-liquidity pools or markets are used as price sources without sufficient safeguards

hints: Enumerate all functions with `onlyOwner`, `onlyAdmin`, or similar access modifiers and assess if they can affect user funds
Check if admin-settable addresses (controller, oracle, fee recipient) can be pointed to malicious contracts that drain funds
Look for "rescue" or "redundant withdrawal" functions that can withdraw deposited user tokens, not just accidentally sent tokens
Verify that critical parameter changes (fee rates, collateral factors, oracle addresses) have timelocks
Check if admin functions can be called multiple times when they should be one-shot (e.g., `applyCover`, `settle`)
Look for admin functions that can bypass pausing, timelocks, or other safety mechanisms designed to protect users
preconditions:
- The protocol has admin/owner/keeper roles with elevated privileges over user funds or protocol parameters
- Privileged functions can directly or indirectly drain user funds, modify critical parameters, or bypass safety controls
- No timelock, multisig requirement, or governance delay is enforced on sensitive admin operations
- Users deposit funds trusting that admin powers are limited, but the actual admin surface area is broader than expected

hints: Identify all external calls (token transfers, ETH sends, callback-triggering operations) in each function
Check if any state variables are modified after these external calls -- this is the classic Checks-Effects-Interactions violation
Look for functions that interact with user-supplied or arbitrary token addresses, as these could be ERC777 tokens with hooks
Check for the absence of `nonReentrant` modifiers on functions that perform external calls
In swap/trade functions, verify whether the quote/price calculation uses reserves that could be stale during a re-entrant call
For multi-step operations (register trade, then swap), check if credited amounts can be inflated by re-entering between steps
preconditions:
- The contract interacts with tokens that have callback mechanisms (ERC777 `tokensReceived`, ERC721 `onERC721Received`, or native ETH transfers to contracts with fallback/receive functions)
- State updates occur after external calls (violation of Checks-Effects-Interactions pattern)
- No reentrancy guard (`nonReentrant` modifier) is applied to the vulnerable function
- The contract uses balance-based accounting that can be manipulated between re-entrant calls

hints: Look for batch NFT purchase/sale functions that calculate royalties using an averaged or equally-divided price rather than per-item valuation
Check if `royaltyInfo()` is called with the correct per-item sale price reflecting the item's weight or individual value
Verify that royalty recipient addresses are validated (not zero address, can receive ETH/tokens)
Check if royalty payments within loops can cause reentrancy or allow malicious recipients to manipulate the transaction
Look for inconsistencies between how ERC-2981 `royaltyInfo` returns are handled versus how the protocol splits fees among multiple recipients
Verify that collections with non-standard `getRoyalties()` implementations are handled correctly
preconditions:
- The protocol distributes royalties or creator fees based on NFT sales (typically implementing ERC-2981)
- Multiple NFTs with different values or different royalty rates are sold in a single batch transaction
- Royalty calculation assumes uniform pricing across all NFTs in a batch, or does not properly weight individual items
- Creator fee recipients can be zero address, contracts that cannot receive ETH, or malicious contracts
- The protocol allows multiple royalty recipients per collection with split logic

hints: Find all `ecrecover` or `ECDSA.recover` calls and trace what data is included in the signed hash
Check if a nonce is included in the signed message and whether it is properly incremented after each use
Verify that the nonce is bound to the correct entity (the signer/identity, not the caller/relayer)
Check if the signature includes the contract address and chain ID to prevent cross-contract and cross-chain replay
Look for signed messages that authorize transfers or state changes -- if the preconditions can recur (e.g., seller re-acquires an NFT), old signatures become replayable
For recovery/governance flows, check if cancelled or executed operations properly invalidate their signatures
preconditions:
- The protocol uses off-chain signatures (EIP-712 or raw ECDSA) for authorization of on-chain actions (e.g., private sales, account recovery, insurance claims)
- Signatures lack a nonce, or the nonce is bound to the wrong entity, or the nonce is not properly incremented/invalidated after use
- The signed message does not include sufficient context to prevent cross-context replay (e.g., missing chain ID, missing contract address, or missing identity binding)
- Previously used signatures are not tracked or invalidated

category_title: Unbounded Loops and Denial of Service
hints: Search for `for` or `while` loops where the upper bound is a storage variable or dynamic array length that can grow over time
Check if the loop contains external calls, storage reads/writes, or other gas-intensive operations that compound the cost
Verify whether the data structure has a removal/pruning mechanism or a hard cap on its size
Look for functions that must iterate through the entire collection to complete (e.g., `redeemMax`, `withdrawAll`, `distributeToAll`)
Check if an attacker can cheaply inflate the data structure (e.g., by placing many small orders or adding entries)
Verify if critical protocol functions (liquidation, settlement, withdrawal) can be permanently blocked by growing the loop beyond block gas limits
preconditions:
- The contract contains loops that iterate over dynamically-sized arrays or data structures that grow without bound
- Users or external actors can append items to these data structures (e.g., orders, recipients, markets, authorized addresses)
- Critical functions (withdrawals, claims, liquidations) depend on iterating through the entire unbounded collection
- No pagination or batch-processing mechanism exists to limit gas consumption per call

category_title: Unsafe External Calls and Token Transfers
hints: Search for `.transfer(` calls on `address payable` -- these are limited to 2300 gas and will fail for many smart contract recipients
Search for `.send(` calls and verify the return value is checked
Look for `IERC20(...).transfer()` and `IERC20(...).transferFrom()` calls without using `SafeERC20` -- some tokens (USDT, BNB) return `false` instead of reverting
Check if failed transfers leave the contract in an inconsistent state (e.g., balance decremented but tokens not actually sent)
Verify that ETH withdrawal patterns work for both EOAs and contract recipients
Look for withdrawal functions that block all withdrawals if one recipient's transfer fails (push vs pull pattern)
preconditions:
- The contract uses `address.transfer()` or `address.send()` to send ETH, which forwards only 2300 gas
- The recipient is or could be a smart contract (multisig, proxy, contract wallet) that requires more than 2300 gas in its fallback/receive function
- Token transfers use raw `transfer()` / `transferFrom()` without checking return values on non-reverting ERC20 tokens
- The contract does not use OpenZeppelin's `SafeERC20` library for token interactions

hints: Check if the contract claims EIP-4626 compliance (in comments, docs, or by inheriting IERC4626) and compare against the full specification

Verify all required EIP-4626 functions exist: `deposit`, `mint`, `withdraw`, `redeem`, `totalAssets`, `convertToShares`, `convertToAssets`, `maxDeposit`, `maxMint`, `maxWithdraw`, `maxRedeem`, `previewDeposit`, `previewMint`, `previewWithdraw`, `previewRedeem`
Check if `max*` functions return 0 when the corresponding operation is disabled or paused
Verify that `preview*` functions account for fees and accurately reflect what the user will receive
Search for `safeApprove` usage with tokens known to revert on non-zero to non-zero approval changes (USDT)
Look for ERC1155 `safeTransferFrom` in loops where one receiver's callback failure blocks all transfers
Check if `totalAssets` correctly tracks all managed assets including those distributed or locked
preconditions:
- Protocol claims to implement EIP-4626 (tokenized vault) but deviates from the specification
- ERC1155 token callbacks (`onERC1155Received`) are not properly handled during batch operations
- Functions specified by the standard are missing, return incorrect values, or have non-conforming signatures
- `maxDeposit`/`maxWithdraw`/`maxMint`/`maxRedeem` do not reflect actual limits (e.g., when paused)
- `totalAssets` does not accurately represent the underlying value managed by the vault
- Token approval patterns use deprecated `safeApprove` that fails for tokens like USDT

hints: Search for `safeTransfer` or `safeTransferFrom` calls where the transferred amount is directly used in subsequent accounting without measuring actual balance change
Look for contracts that receive tokens and then forward the same nominal amount to another address
Identify deposit functions that credit users with the input amount rather than the actual received amount
Check if the protocol documents which tokens it supports and whether fee-on-transfer tokens are explicitly excluded
Look for patterns where `balanceOf` is not checked before and after transfers
preconditions:
- Protocol accepts arbitrary ERC20 tokens or tokens known to have (or potentially enable) transfer fees (e.g., USDT, USDC with future fee capability)
- Code assumes that the amount sent in `transfer`/`transferFrom` equals the amount received
- Tokens with fee-on-transfer are used in deposit, withdrawal, or internal accounting flows
- The contract acts as an intermediary that receives tokens and then forwards them to another recipient

hints: Search for pool withdrawal functions (`exitPool`, `returnFunds`, `finalize`) that lack minimum output amount parameters
Identify deposit functions without price boundary or slippage parameters
Look for state transition functions (timeout, liquidation, fraud proof) that reward the caller and are front-runnable
Check for two-step processes (approve + action) where the action can be front-run after observing the approval
Search for `block.timestamp` comparisons in time-sensitive operations that are observable before execution
Look for functions that modify AMM weights or swap fees without cooldown periods
preconditions:
- Protocol performs on-chain swaps, deposits, or withdrawals involving AMM pools without slippage protection
- Finalization or settlement transactions are publicly visible in the mempool before execution
- No minimum output amount or price boundary checks on deposits/withdrawals
- State transitions (e.g., fraud proofs, timeouts, liquidations) can be observed and preempted
- Proposal sponsorship or approval operations involve token transfers that can be intercepted

hints: Search for constructors and `initialize` functions, then check if all address parameters are validated against `address(0)` and cross-checked with related contracts
Identify numeric parameters (timestamps, fees, periods) without minimum value checks
Look for string parameters without empty-string checks where the value has functional significance
Check if array lengths are cross-validated between cooperating contracts (e.g., validator token count vs vault token count)
Search for `setSwapFee`, `setManager`, or similar setter functions without adequate input bounds
Look for timestamp parameters not validated against `type(uint32).max` for packed storage compatibility
Verify that signature/nonce validation exists for replay-sensitive operations
preconditions:
- Constructor or initializer parameters are not validated against expected ranges or invariants
- Functions accept addresses, timestamps, or amounts without sanity checks
- Deployment is performed by external parties (e.g., treasury partners) who may misconfigure parameters
- Related contracts (validator, vault) are deployed independently without cross-validation
- Critical configuration values can be set to zero or to values that break protocol invariants

hints: Search for `latestRoundData()` calls and verify all return values are checked (`roundId`, `price`, `updatedAt`, `answeredInRound`)
Look for hardcoded decimal assumptions (e.g., `* 1e10` or `/ 1e8`) instead of dynamic `decimals()` calls
Check if `updatedAt` or `answeredInRound` is validated against staleness thresholds
For L2 deployments (Arbitrum, Optimism), verify sequencer uptime feed is consulted
Identify price ratio calculations between two feeds and verify decimal normalization handles different precisions
Check if `price > 0` is validated (negative or zero price from oracle)
Look for `minTokenOut` set to 0 in redemptions that rely on oracle-reported exchange rates
preconditions:
- Protocol relies on Chainlink or other external price oracles for critical operations (liquidations, payouts, collateral valuation)
- Oracle decimal handling is hardcoded or assumed to be uniform across all feeds
- No staleness checks on oracle responses (missing `updatedAt` validation)
- L2 sequencer uptime feed is not checked when deployed on rollups (Arbitrum, Optimism)
- Multiple oracle feeds are combined (e.g., PegOracle using two feeds) with incorrect decimal normalization

hints: Search for arithmetic expressions where division precedes multiplication (e.g., `a / b * c` instead of `a * c / b`)
Look for reward rate calculations with large denominators (durations in seconds) and verify the rounding impact
Identify interest accrual functions and check if they are callable at arbitrary frequency, allowing compounded rounding loss
Search for `/ totalSupply()` or similar divisors that can be zero
Look for price ratio calculations between feeds with different decimals where normalization may lose precision
Check if intermediate calculations use sufficient precision (e.g., multiplying by `1e18` before dividing)
Identify functions where returned values pass through multiple division operations
preconditions:
- Protocol performs division before multiplication in fee, reward, or price calculations
- Reward rates are computed over long durations (years), amplifying per-second rounding errors
- Interest accrual functions can be called frequently, each call rounding down small increments
- Price ratios are computed from oracle feeds with different decimal precisions
- Division by zero is possible when pool is empty or total supply is zero

hints: Search for `onlyOwner` or `onlyAdmin` functions that can transfer, migrate, or redirect user funds
Identify `setController`, `setManager`, `setKeeper` functions that lack timelocks or multi-sig requirements
Look for `sweep` or `recoverERC20` functions without token blacklisting (should exclude pool tokens, underlying assets)
Check if registry/factory admin can register arbitrary addresses that gain privileged access to vaults
Verify that manager and owner roles have properly separated capabilities
Look for functions that can bypass withdrawal limits, fee payments, or notice periods
Search for `migrate` patterns that transfer all funds to a new address controlled by a single role
preconditions:
- Owner, admin, controller, or manager role has excessive unilateral power over user funds
- Critical functions like `setController`, `sweep`, or `withdrawRedundant` lack proper restrictions
- Role can be changed without timelock, allowing instant privilege transfer
- No separation of concerns between operational roles (manager) and custodial roles (treasury/owner)
- Registry or factory admin can register malicious contracts that gain vault access

hints: Search for external calls (`.transfer`, `.safeTransfer`, `.call`, swap router invocations) that occur before state variable updates
Identify functions missing `nonReentrant` modifiers that interact with external contracts
Look for user-controlled swap paths or exchange data parameters that could include malicious intermediary tokens
Check if ERC777 or ERC1155 tokens with callbacks are supported without reentrancy guards
Verify that all entry points sharing state (vault, router, request manager) use a common reentrancy lock
Examine snapshot-based accounting (balance before/after patterns) where reentrancy can corrupt the snapshot
preconditions:
- Contract performs external calls (token transfers, swaps, callbacks) mid-execution before updating internal state
- Entry points lack `nonReentrant` modifiers or use inconsistent reentrancy guards
- ERC777, ERC1155, or arbitrary tokens with transfer callbacks are accepted
- Swap paths allow user-controlled intermediary tokens (e.g., Uniswap V2 multi-hop)
- Protocol interacts with external DeFi protocols that may trigger callbacks

hints: Search for `notifyRewardAmount` and check if the balance validation accounts for already-earned but unclaimed rewards
Look for reward rate calculations that use `rewardsDuration` as denominator - verify rounding impact over long durations
Identify shared reward token contracts serving multiple pools and check if balance queries are pool-specific
Check if staking/reward claiming is properly gated by epoch start/end timestamps
Look for `recoverERC20` functions that can extract reward tokens, acting as a backdoor
Verify that reward distribution is paused or rolled over correctly between epochs
Search for precision loss in `rewardPerToken` calculations where small `totalSupply` causes significant rounding
preconditions:
- Protocol distributes rewards to stakers/liquidity providers via time-based reward rate calculations
- Reward tokens are held in a shared contract serving multiple pools or epochs
- `notifyRewardAmount` or equivalent function can be called repeatedly, diluting existing reward periods
- Reward balance checks compare contract balance to future obligations without accounting for unclaimed rewards
- Staking/unstaking is not properly gated around epoch boundaries or reward distribution events

hints: Search for `for` loops that iterate over storage arrays without a fixed upper bound or pagination
Look for array-shifting patterns in remove operations (O(n) complexity per removal)
Identify user-facing functions that can grow arrays without cost limits or size caps
Check if critical protocol functions (compensation, liquidation, reward distribution) iterate unbounded arrays
Search for `while` loops in queue processing without iteration limits
Look for delegation or staking patterns where minimum amounts are not enforced
Estimate gas costs for worst-case array sizes and compare against block gas limits
preconditions:
- Protocol iterates over dynamically-sized arrays or linked lists without bounding the iteration count
- User actions (deposits, delegations, queue entries) can grow arrays that are iterated in critical functions
- Queue processing, reward distribution, or index updates enumerate all elements in a single transaction
- An attacker can cheaply add entries to inflate the array size (e.g., small delegations, trivial queue entries)
- Block gas limits constrain maximum computation per transaction

hints: Search for queue data structures (`nextRequestId`, `lastRequestId`, `queue[]`) and trace all operations that modify queue pointers
Identify cancellation flows and verify that the queue head pointer (`nextRequestId`) is properly advanced past cancelled entries
Look for `_removeFromQueue` or similar functions that use array shifting - check if the final element is properly removed
Check if multiple fulfillment paths (manual, instant, batch) independently check liquidity without coordinating via `reservedLiquidity`
Search for unbounded loops in queue processing that iterate over all entries
Verify that partial fills update request state consistently (shares, sharePrice, claimableAssets)
Look for `fulfillCancelRedeemRequest` that calls `_reduce` with potentially stale or mismatched data
preconditions:
- Protocol implements a FIFO withdrawal or redemption queue for processing user exit requests
- Queue state can be desynchronized by cancellations, partial fills, or concurrent operations
- Queue iteration is unbounded or grows linearly with pending requests
- Remove operations do not properly clean up queue entries, leaving ghost entries
- Multiple fulfillment paths (manual, instant, queued) can conflict and double-count state

hints: Check Dutch auction price calculations for potential underflow when `dropPerSecond * elapsedTime` exceeds `startPrice`
Look for sale finalization logic that allows additional calls after the sale has ended (especially `buy(0)` or similar edge cases)
Verify that refund mechanisms work correctly when not all items are sold -- funds should not be permanently locked
Check if `selfdestruct` is used in auction contracts where buyers might send ETH after destruction
Identify auction parameter setter functions that lack checks for active auction state
Look for missing deadline/expiration checks that allow purchases after the intended sale period
Verify that fee calculations are consistent between the sale price charged to buyers and the amount distributed to receivers
preconditions:
- Protocol implements on-chain auctions (English, Dutch/LPDA, fixed price sales) for NFTs or tokens
- Auction settlement or price calculations depend on parameters that can underflow, overflow, or be manipulated
- Auction end-of-sale logic allows edge cases where refunds, fees, or proceeds are mishandled
- The auction contract holds ETH/tokens from multiple bidders and distributions depend on correct accounting
- Auction parameters (duration, price drop rate, start price) can be set to values that cause arithmetic issues

hints: Identify all `onlyOwner`, `onlyAdmin`, `onlyOperator`, or similar access-controlled functions
Check if any admin function can directly transfer, burn, or mint tokens affecting user balances
Look for `setOperator`, `setImplementation`, `setTokenAddress`, or similar functions that change critical addresses without a timelock
Verify ownership transfer uses a two-step pattern (propose + accept) rather than single-step
Check if the protocol has a single EOA owner rather than a multisig or governance contract
Look for upgrade mechanisms (UUPS, Transparent Proxy, Diamond) that lack a timelock between proposal and execution
Identify functions that allow the admin to change fee parameters, reward rates, or slashing conditions with immediate effect
preconditions:
- A single admin/owner address holds elevated privileges that can modify critical protocol parameters
- No timelock, multisig, or governance mechanism guards admin-only functions
- Admin can directly move user funds, change token addresses, modify fee structures, or pause the protocol
- Protocol upgrade mechanisms (proxy, diamond) allow the admin to change implementation without delay
- No two-step ownership transfer process exists, allowing accidental or malicious ownership changes

hints: Check how `delegate()` handles the case where `delegates[account]` is `address(0)` (never delegated) -- votes should not be created from nothing when self-delegating
Verify that `_afterTokenTransfer` correctly handles delegation for the receiving address when `delegates[to] == address(0)`
Look for circular dependencies where undelegating requires the delegate to be inactive, but the delegate can always remain active
Check if delegation state is properly cleaned up when tokens are burned
Verify that explicit self-delegation and implicit self-delegation (address(0)) are treated consistently
Look for missing validation that prevents delegation to address(0)
preconditions:
- Protocol implements vote delegation where token/NFT holders can delegate their voting power to another address
- Delegation state tracking does not properly handle edge cases around self-delegation, address(0), and implicit vs explicit delegation
- Token transfers move both the token and delegated voting power, but delegation bookkeeping is inconsistent
- A delegate can perform actions (create proposals, vote) that lock the delegator's ability to undelegate or unstake

hints: Search for `.transfer(` and `.send(` patterns in the codebase -- these always forward only 2300 gas
Check if ETH recipients can be arbitrary addresses (user-supplied) that may be smart contracts
Look for withdrawal patterns where failure to send ETH causes funds to become permanently locked
Verify if the contract has a fallback mechanism for failed ETH transfers (e.g., WETH wrapping or pull-based withdrawal)
Check assembly blocks for hardcoded gas stipends in `call` instructions
preconditions:
- Contract uses `.transfer()` or `.send()` to send ETH, which forwards only 2300 gas
- Recipients may be smart contracts with `receive()` or `fallback()` functions that consume more than 2300 gas
- Recipients may be multisig wallets, proxy contracts, or contracts that perform state updates on ETH receipt
- Future EVM gas cost changes (e.g., EIP-2929 storage access cost increases) may cause previously-working transfers to fail

hints: Identify checkpoint/snapshot mechanisms and check whether `getAtBlock()` or `getPastVotes()` returns the first or last value when multiple checkpoints exist in the same block
Look for staking/deposit functions that allow same-block entry and exit without a minimum lock period
Check if voting power or reward calculations reference checkpoint values that could be inflated via flash-loaned tokens
Identify price oracle calls that use spot prices (AMM reserves, pool balances) without TWAP or manipulation resistance
Verify if collateral status checks use instantaneous prices that can be moved by large flash-loan-funded trades
Look for the pattern: `transferFrom` -> `writeCheckpoint` -> `transfer` all possible in a single transaction
preconditions:
- Protocol uses checkpoints or snapshots based on `block.number` or `block.timestamp` for determining voting power, staking weight, or collateral value
- Checkpoint lookups return values from the first checkpoint in a block rather than the last
- Users can acquire tokens, perform an action (stake, vote, deposit), and return tokens all within a single transaction
- No minimum holding period or cross-block delay is enforced between deposit and withdrawal
- Flash loan liquidity is available for the relevant token

hints: Identify two-step operations where step 1 creates an approval/offer visible on-chain and step 2 consumes it -- check if step 2 has `msg.sender` validation
Look for admin/penalty functions (`slash`, `pause`, `unauthorize`) that modify user state -- these can be observed in the mempool and front-run
Check if functions that consume approvals or allowances verify that `msg.sender` is the original approver/owner
Look for state-dependent validations (hash checks, balance checks) where another party can modify the state between the user's transaction submission and execution
Verify whether commit-reveal patterns or private submission channels are used for sensitive operations
Check if sandwich attack vectors exist where an attacker can profit by placing transactions before and after a user's transaction
preconditions:
- A transaction's outcome depends on on-chain state that can change between submission and execution
- An operation requires a two-step process (approve then act) where the second step can be intercepted
- Slashing, penalty, or administrative functions are callable and observable in the mempool
- No commit-reveal scheme or private mempool is used for sensitive operations
- Token approvals or offers are visible on-chain before the intended recipient acts

category_title: Gas Griefing and EIP-150 63/64 Rule Exploitation
hints: Search for `try/catch` blocks where the `catch` branch performs critical state changes (pause, slash, fail job) -- these are vulnerable to deliberate OOG in the `try` branch
Calculate the minimum gas needed for the `catch` block and check if 1/64 of reasonable gas limits provides enough
Look for `gasleft()` checks before external calls that do not account for the 63/64 rule (the check should require `gasleft() * 63 / 64 >= gasLimit`)
Identify unbounded loops or recursive calls on data structures with no maximum depth/length
Check cross-chain messaging where `gasLimit` is user-supplied without an upper bound relative to the destination chain's block gas limit
Look for functions callable by anyone (no access control) that could be called with a carefully crafted gas limit
preconditions:
- A function uses `try/catch` to handle failures from an external call, with critical state changes (like pausing) in the `catch` block
- The external call's gas consumption is variable and can be high under certain conditions (e.g., batch minting with high founder percentages)
- The 63/64 gas forwarding rule (EIP-150) can be exploited to make the external call fail with out-of-gas while leaving enough gas for the `catch` block to execute
- Functions with unbounded loops or recursive calls operate on user-controlled or dynamically growing data
- Cross-chain gas parameters are set by the user without proper validation or upper bounds

hints: Check if `propose()` snapshots quorum or threshold using `block.timestamp` or `block.number` of the current transaction rather than a past checkpoint
Look for `_writeCheckpoint` implementations that create a new entry on every call rather than updating the latest entry when `block.timestamp` matches
Identify if `getPastVotes(account, timestamp)` binary search can return stale/wrong data when multiple checkpoints share the same timestamp
Check if NFT transfers allow a user to vote, transfer the NFT, and vote again from another wallet in the same block as proposal creation
Verify that delegation properly handles the case where `delegates[account] == address(0)` (implicit self-delegation) and explicit self-delegation to avoid double-counting
Confirm that `unstake()` decreases voting power by the same amount that `stake()` increased it, even if parameters like `baseVotes` or multipliers changed between calls
preconditions:
- Protocol uses on-chain governance with NFT-based or token-based voting power
- Voting power snapshots use the same block/timestamp as the proposal creation, allowing same-block manipulation
- Checkpoint mechanism does not correctly handle multiple state changes within a single block
- Delegation logic allows self-delegation to double voting weight, or transferring tokens to re-vote
- Quorum calculations use current total supply rather than a checkpointed historical value

hints: Search for `latestRoundData()` calls without sequencer uptime validation on L2 deployments
Check if oracle timeout handling returns zero prices that are subsequently used in trade calculations
Look for AMM spot price usage (`getReserves`, `getSpotPrice`) without TWAP or manipulation resistance
Identify `refresh()` or price update functions that revert entirely when an oracle is deprecated or unavailable
Verify that oracle price answers are checked for `answer > 0` and `updatedAt` is within acceptable bounds
Check if round completeness is validated (`answeredInRound >= roundId`)
preconditions:
- Protocol relies on external price oracles (Chainlink, Curve, AMM spot prices) for collateral valuation, liquidations, or trading
- Oracle staleness checks are missing or use overly generous heartbeat timeouts
- Protocol is deployed on L2 (Arbitrum, Optimism) without checking L2 sequencer uptime
- Price feeds can return stale, zero, or deprecated data without the protocol reverting
- AMM spot prices are used without TWAP protection, making them vulnerable to manipulation

hints: Search for arithmetic expressions where division precedes multiplication: `(a / b) * c` instead of `(a * c) / b`
Check calculations involving tokens with different decimals -- ensure decimal normalization before comparison or arithmetic
Look for share/fraction computations where small numerators divided by large denominators yield 0
Identify downcasting operations (`uint80(value)`, `uint48(amount)`) without bounds checking
Verify that fixed-point math libraries handle edge cases where intermediate values overflow or underflow
Check if reward distribution calculations can produce 0 rewards for legitimate stakers due to truncation
preconditions:
- Arithmetic operations involve division before multiplication, causing intermediate precision loss
- Token amounts are computed using ratios where the numerator can be significantly smaller than the denominator
- Tokens with different decimals (e.g., 6 vs 18) are used in the same calculation without normalization
- Share/fraction calculations involve integer division that truncates remainally-significant remainders
- Price or reward calculations use fixed-point math where scaling factors are insufficient

hints: Identify all external calls: `.call{}()`, `.transfer()`, `.send()`, `IERC20.transfer()`, `safeTransferFrom()`, and any interface calls to untrusted addresses
Check if state variables (balances, counters, flags, timestamps) are updated after the external call rather than before
Look for custom reentrancy guards that reuse business-logic variables (e.g., timestamps, status flags) instead of dedicated lock variables
Verify whether a privileged role can reset the guard variable during execution via a separate function
Check if the contract interacts with callback tokens (ERC777, ERC721 with `onERC721Received`, or contracts with `receive`/`fallback`) that hand control to an arbitrary address
Confirm there is no `nonReentrant` modifier (OpenZeppelin `ReentrancyGuard`) on the vulnerable function
preconditions:
- Contract makes external calls (ETH transfers, ERC20/ERC777 token transfers, cross-contract calls) before updating internal state
- Reentrancy guard is either missing, uses a non-standard implementation, or can be bypassed through a secondary entry point
- Callback tokens (ERC777 `tokensToSend`/`tokensReceived`, ERC721 `onERC721Received`, or ETH `receive`/`fallback`) transfer execution control to an untrusted address
- A state variable used as a reentrancy guard can be reset by a privileged role during execution

hints: Check `rewardPerToken()` for periods where `totalSupply == 0` -- rewards accrued during these periods are typically lost forever
Look for `claimRewards` functions that accept epoch/period parameters without validating they are in the past
Identify admin functions that change reward parameters (rate, weight, duration) without first calling `updateReward()` or equivalent settlement
Check if reward token addresses can equal the staking token address, creating circular dependencies
Verify that duplicate reward tokens cannot be added to `extraRewards` arrays (double-counting rewards)
Look for rounding errors in `notifyRewardAmount` where `reward / duration * duration < reward`, leaving dust locked
preconditions:
- Protocol distributes rewards proportionally to stakers, LPs, or token holders over time
- Reward distribution uses a `rewardPerTokenStored` accumulator pattern where rewards accrue per unit of staked tokens
- There are periods where `totalSupply == 0` (no stakers), causing rewards to be permanently locked
- Reward claiming logic does not enforce epoch boundaries, allowing claims for future or past epochs
- Staking reward weight can be updated by admin without first settling accrued rewards

hints: Search for `selfdestruct` usage in any contract that holds user funds or serves as a proxy implementation
Check if implementation contracts behind proxies are initialized in the constructor or have initializer protection
Look for sale/auction contracts that use `selfdestruct` for cleanup -- verify no function can be called after destruction
Identify `delegatecall` in contracts that could be targeted with a `selfdestruct` payload
Check if the protocol relies on `selfdestruct` behavior that will change or be removed under EIP-4758
Look for race conditions where `selfdestruct` and subsequent calls can happen in the same transaction
preconditions:
- Contract uses `selfdestruct` to distribute remaining funds after a sale or to clean up contracts
- An implementation contract behind a proxy is left uninitialized, allowing anyone to become the owner
- The implementation contract has a function that calls `selfdestruct`, which destroys the logic for all proxies
- EIP-4758 (deprecation of `selfdestruct`) is not accounted for in contracts that rely on this opcode
- A `buy()` or similar function can be called after `selfdestruct`, sending ETH to the destroyed address

hints: Search for `ecrecover` calls and verify the returned address is checked against `address(0)`
Look for signature verification without EIP-712 domain separator (missing `chainId`, `verifyingContract`)
Check if nonces are used and incremented to prevent signature replay
Identify hash construction using `abi.encodePacked` with variable-length types that can create collisions
Look for truncated hash usage (e.g., `bytes4(keccak256(...))`) in security-critical contexts
Verify that multisig threshold checks properly handle the case where `ecrecover` returns address(0)
preconditions:
- Contract uses `ecrecover` for signature verification without checking for `address(0)` return
- Hash encoding uses non-standard or incorrect packing that leads to collisions or incorrect verification
- Signature-based authorization lacks replay protection (missing nonce, chain ID, or domain separator)
- Multisig or threshold signature schemes rely on signer validation that can be fooled by zero-address returns

hints: Search for direct calls to `IERC20.transfer()`, `IERC20.transferFrom()`, and `IERC20.approve()` without wrapping in OpenZeppelin's `SafeERC20` library
Check if the protocol uses `require(token.transfer(...))` pattern which fails for tokens that do not return `bool` (USDT on mainnet)
Look for deposits that record `amount` parameter directly without measuring the actual balance change (`balanceBefore` / `balanceAfter` pattern)
Check if the protocol documentation or code assumes all tokens are standard ERC20 compliant while accepting arbitrary token addresses
Look for `approve` calls that do not first set allowance to 0 before setting a new value (some tokens like USDT require this)
preconditions:
- Contract interacts with arbitrary or user-supplied ERC20 tokens
- Token transfer functions (`transfer`, `transferFrom`, `approve`) are called using the standard `IERC20` interface without wrapping in safe helpers
- The protocol does not account for fee-on-transfer, rebasing, or deflationary token mechanics
- Some supported tokens do not return a boolean on `transfer`/`transferFrom` (e.g., USDT, BNB)

hints: Search for explicit type casts to smaller types: `uint80(...)`, `uint48(...)`, `uint32(...)`, `int8(...)`, etc.
Check if the source value can realistically exceed the target type's range
Look for accumulated values in loops or across multiple calls that could overflow the smaller type
Identify user-supplied values (`msg.value`, function parameters) that are downcast without validation
Check for `int8(uint8(...))` patterns where unsigned values in the upper half become negative
Verify whether Solidity 0.8+ checked arithmetic applies (it does NOT prevent truncation in explicit casts)
preconditions:
- Contract casts user-supplied or computed values from a larger integer type to a smaller one (e.g., `uint256` to `uint80`, `uint48`, `int8`)
- No bounds checking is performed before the cast, allowing silent truncation
- The truncated value is used in critical computations (pricing, amounts, accounting)
- Solidity version < 0.8 is used (no automatic overflow checks), or explicit `unchecked` blocks are present
- Partial order fills or iterative operations can cause accumulated values to overflow smaller types

hints: Look for `onBehalf` parameters in repay/liquidate/redeem functions and verify they are validated against the position owner
Check if auction claim or NFT transfer functions verify that `msg.sender` is the legitimate winner/owner
Verify that L2 deployments include sequencer uptime checks before oracle consumption
Look for functions that modify other users' positions without proper authorization checks
Check if administrative functions (pause, config changes) have proper access control modifiers
preconditions:
- Protocol has functions that should restrict who can call them or which parameters can be used
- Actions like repaying on behalf of others, claiming NFT collateral, or modifying position modes should validate the caller's relationship to the position
- L2 deployments depend on sequencer availability for oracle-related access patterns
- Parameter validation (e.g., `onBehalf` matching the position owner) may be missing or insufficient

hints: Search for `balanceOf` calls and verify the address and token being queried matches what the function actually needs
Check supply/debt share arithmetic: minting shares should increase totals, burning should decrease
Verify that "full" operations (full repay, full liquidation, full withdrawal) handle rounding remainder correctly
Check if share-to-asset conversion uses the correct index (liquidityIndex for supply, borrowIndex for debt)
Look for operations that modify cached values but forget to write them back to storage
Verify that interest rate calculations use post-operation share counts, not pre-operation
preconditions:
- Protocol uses share-based accounting to track deposits, borrows, or collateral positions
- Multiple operations (supply, borrow, repay, withdraw, liquidate) modify the same accounting variables
- Share-to-asset or asset-to-share conversions depend on dynamic indices (liquidityIndex, borrowIndex)
- Balance queries (`balanceOf`) may target the wrong address or wrong token when interacting with wrapped/vault tokens

hints: Check if `borrow` functions enforce a minimum borrow amount in USD or ETH value terms
Verify that minimum deposit checks cannot be bypassed by depositing then withdrawing
Look for bad debt handling after liquidation -- if the debt exceeds collateral, is the remainder zeroed out and distributed?
Check if the protocol has a mechanism to socialize bad debt across all suppliers (e.g., reducing the liquidity index)
Verify that protocol pause/unpause does not create windows where compounding interest pushes positions into bad debt without liquidation opportunity
Check if collateral parameter changes (LTV, liquidation threshold) can instantly make healthy positions liquidatable without grace period
preconditions:
- Lending protocol allows borrowing against collateral with a liquidation mechanism
- No minimum borrow amount is enforced, or existing minimums can be bypassed
- Bad debt (debt exceeding collateral value) can accumulate without being socialized or written off
- Liquidation incentives may be insufficient to cover gas costs for small positions
- Protocol pauses or config changes can create windows where positions become unliquidatable

hints: Check if Balancer `joinPool`/`exitPool` userData encoding matches the actual JoinKind/ExitKind being used
Verify that function signatures for external protocol calls match the actual ABI (check for renamed or deprecated functions)
Look for hardcoded peg assumptions (stETH:ETH = 1:1, PT:underlying = 1:1) that may break during market stress
Check if the protocol handles external protocol version changes (e.g., Uniswap V2 vs V3, Pendle V2 router changes)
Verify that external protocol constraints (min/max amounts, withdrawal delays, whitelisting) are respected
Check if Convex/Aura wrapper contracts handle dynamic addition of reward tokens without corrupting accounting
preconditions:
- Protocol integrates with external DeFi protocols (Aura, Convex, Balancer, Curve, Lido, EtherFi, Pendle, Uniswap) for yield, collateral management, or token swaps
- External protocol APIs have specific requirements (function signatures, parameter encoding, token standards) that must be exactly matched
- External protocols may have version changes, deprecated APIs, or chain-specific behaviors
- Assumptions about external token behavior (1:1 peg, constant rate, non-rebasing) may not hold in all market conditions

category_title: Interest Accrual and Rate Model Errors
hints: Check if `setDebtInterestApr` or `setReserveFactor` calls `accrueInterest()` or `updateState()` before modifying the parameter
Verify that bad debt positions stop accruing interest (debt above collateral value should be frozen or written off)
Check if compound vs linear interest formulas create a surplus that is properly handled
Verify that treasury/reserve share calculations do not double-dip (earning both fee allocation AND supply interest)
Look for utilization-based rate models where stale debt (from bad debt) distorts the utilization calculation
Check if the freeze/pause mechanism stops interest accrual or if frozen debt continues compounding
preconditions:
- Protocol uses time-weighted interest accrual with periodic state updates
- Interest rates depend on utilization (borrowed / total) or are set via governance
- Rate model parameters (APR, reserve factor, fee rate) can be changed by admin
- Interest should stop accruing on positions that no longer have active debt
- Compound vs linear interest formulas may be used for different sides (borrow vs supply)

hints: In `executeRepay`, check if `updateInterestRates` is called BEFORE `repayDebt` -- rates will use stale (higher) debt
In `executeWithdraw`, check if `updateInterestRates` is called BEFORE the supply is reduced
In `executeLiquidationCall`, verify that rates are updated AFTER all token transfers (including fee transfers to treasury)
Check if config changes (reserve factor, rate model) call `updateState()` first to settle accrued interest before applying the new configuration
Verify the `cache` object used in rate calculations reflects the post-operation state, not the pre-operation state
preconditions:
- Protocol follows the Aave-style pattern of updating interest rate indices before or after balance-changing operations (supply, borrow, repay, withdraw, liquidate)
- Interest rate calculation depends on current utilization (totalDebt / totalLiquidity)
- Rate indices (liquidityIndex, borrowIndex) are updated via `updateState()` or similar, and rate recalculation uses cached debt/supply values
- Multiple operations (repay, withdraw, liquidate) modify both balances and rates in the same transaction

hints: In `executeRepay`, check if `updateInterestRates` is called BEFORE `repayDebt` -- rates will use stale (higher) debt
In `executeWithdraw`, check if `updateInterestRates` is called BEFORE the supply is reduced
In `executeLiquidationCall`, verify that rates are updated AFTER all token transfers (including fee transfers to treasury)
Check if config changes (reserve factor, rate model) call `updateState()` first to settle accrued interest before applying the new configuration
Verify the `cache` object used in rate calculations reflects the post-operation state, not the pre-operation state
preconditions:
- Protocol follows the Aave-style pattern of updating interest rate indices before or after balance-changing operations (supply, borrow, repay, withdraw, liquidate)
- Interest rate calculation depends on current utilization (totalDebt / totalLiquidity)
- Rate indices (liquidityIndex, borrowIndex) are updated via `updateState()` or similar, and rate recalculation uses cached debt/supply values
- Multiple operations (repay, withdraw, liquidate) modify both balances and rates in the same transaction

hints: Check if `executeLiquidationCall` or equivalent burns/withdraws collateral shares matching the TOTAL amount leaving the pool (collateral to liquidator + protocol fees + any bonus)
Verify that interest rates and indices are updated AFTER all balance changes during liquidation
Look for liquidation functions that transfer underlying tokens directly -- if the pool can be drained by other borrows, liquidation can be DOSed
Check if the debt position is fully zeroed out after "full liquidation" -- partial remainder can leave dust debt
Verify that borrowing flags (e.g., `setBorrowing(false)`) are only set when debt is truly zero
Check if liquidation can be front-run by the borrower (e.g., tiny repayments that change share counts)
preconditions:
- Protocol implements a liquidation mechanism to close undercollateralized positions
- Liquidation involves seizing collateral, repaying debt, and potentially distributing fees or bonuses
- Accounting state (debt shares, supply shares, interest rates) must be updated atomically alongside collateral/debt transfers
- Multiple actors (liquidators, borrowers, protocol treasury) have claims on the same collateral pool

hints: Check if multi-step withdrawal processes validate external protocol constraints (min/max amounts) at initiation time, not just finalization
Look for interest model discrepancies between supply-side (linear) and borrow-side (compound) where the surplus has no extraction mechanism
Verify that all token transfer paths have corresponding withdrawal/rescue paths -- if tokens can enter but not exit, they are trapped
Check if liquidation or fee collection functions can leave tokens in intermediate states with no recovery path
Look for `rescueTokens` or `sweep` functions and verify they cover all stuck-token scenarios
Verify that pause/unpause mechanics do not permanently trap in-flight operations
preconditions:
- Protocol has multi-step withdrawal processes (initiate, wait cooldown, finalize)
- External protocol interactions may have minimum/maximum amount requirements that differ from the lending protocol's constraints
- Token rescue functions may be broken, absent, or unable to handle edge cases
- Unclaimable surplus can build up from interest model discrepancies (compound vs linear) or fee accounting gaps

hints: Search for `safeTransferFrom` or `transferFrom` calls where the input `amount` parameter is used directly in accounting without measuring actual `balanceOf` change

Look for tokens that may have different behavior on different chains (e.g., USDT on Ethereum does not return bool)
preconditions:

- Token may implement fee-on-transfer mechanics (deflationary tokens), rebasing mechanics, or non-standard return values
- Protocol uses `balanceOf` checks or input amount parameters to track received amounts without measuring actual balance changes
- Protocol does not maintain an explicit whitelist of supported token types

hints: Check if a single heartbeat constant is used for multiple Chainlink feeds with different actual update frequencies
Verify that L2 deployments check the sequencer uptime feed before consuming price data
Look for LP token price calculations that query both balances and totalSupply -- check if either can be manipulated during a callback
Verify that `latestRoundData()` return values are fully validated: `price > 0`, `updatedAt != 0`, `answeredInRound >= roundId`
Check that oracle decimal precision is normalized consistently (e.g., all prices scaled to 1e18 or 1e8)
Look for deprecated Chainlink APIs like `latestAnswer()` instead of `latestRoundData()`
preconditions:
- Protocol relies on external oracle price feeds (Chainlink, API3, TWAP, custom LP oracles) for collateral valuation, liquidation thresholds, or position health checks
- Price feeds may have different heartbeats, decimal precisions, or staleness thresholds
- Protocol may operate on L2 networks where sequencer uptime affects oracle reliability
- Oracle queries may be performed inside view functions that can be manipulated via reentrancy

category_title: Position Health Check and Valuation Errors
hints: Check if `getPositionValue` can revert when any asset in the position lacks a price oracle -- this can brick all health-check-dependent operations
Verify that the same collateral valuation method (weighted vs bare) is used consistently across borrow, withdraw, and liquidation health checks
Look for operations that modify position state without a subsequent health check
Check if pending rewards are included in collateral value but can be manipulated (claimed, donated, or inflated)
Verify that health checks use up-to-date interest indices, not stale cached values
Check if decimals mismatch between oracle price and token decimals corrupts position valuation
preconditions:
- Protocol calculates position health (collateral value vs debt value) to determine if a position is liquidatable
- Position valuation incorporates oracle prices, collateral factors, and accrued interest
- Health checks are performed during borrows, withdrawals, and liquidations
- Position value may include non-trivial components like pending rewards, LP token values, or vault shares

hints: Search for `mulDiv(a, b, c)` or `a * b / c` patterns where the division is performed before a subsequent multiplication -- precision is lost in the intermediate step
Check if the protocol handles tokens with different decimal places (6, 8, 18) and verify that decimal normalization is applied consistently
Look for price feed consumption that assumes a fixed decimal output (e.g., always 8 or always 18 decimals)
Check share/asset conversion functions for edge cases where rounding direction matters (deposit should round down shares, withdraw should round up assets owed)
Verify that fee calculations involving basis points or percentages preserve sufficient precision through the computation chain
preconditions:
- Protocol performs arithmetic operations on token amounts with varying decimal precisions (e.g., USDC with 6 decimals, WETH with 18 decimals)
- Share-to-asset or asset-to-share conversions use division that can truncate intermediate results
- Price feeds may return values in different decimal scales (8 or 18 decimals) that must be normalized
- Mathematical formulas involve division before multiplication, losing precision in intermediate steps

hints: Check for external calls (token transfers, ETH sends, cross-contract calls) that occur BEFORE state updates in the same function
Look for view functions used in price calculations that read from external contracts susceptible to state manipulation during callbacks
Verify that ALL state-mutating functions have reentrancy guards (not just some)
Check if the protocol integrates with Balancer, Curve, or other protocols known to have callback-based reentrancy vectors
Look for `receive()` or `fallback()` functions in contracts that handle ETH, as these enable reentrancy during ETH transfers
Check if initialization functions are protected against reentrancy (OpenZeppelin's `initializer` before v4.3.2 had reentrancy issues)
preconditions:
- Contract makes external calls (token transfers, callbacks, cross-contract calls) before updating internal state
- Protocol interacts with tokens that support callbacks (ERC777, ERC721, native ETH) or external DeFi protocols with callback hooks
- View functions that calculate prices or balances can be called during the reentrancy window
- Protocol does not use reentrancy guards on all state-mutating functions or uses them inconsistently

hints: Check if removing a reward token from the list claims all pending rewards first
Verify that ALL reward tokens (including extra/bonus rewards) are forwarded to the end user, not left in intermediate contracts
Look for reward debt/accumulator updates that happen before the actual token transfer -- if the transfer fails, rewards are lost
Check if liquidation properly resets or transfers the reward accumulator for the liquidated position
Verify that rewards deposited when no stakers exist are handled (they should be queued, not lost)
Check if reward tokens can be stolen by re-entrancy during the claim process
preconditions:
- Protocol distributes reward tokens (CRV, AURA, COMP, etc.) to users based on their staked or supplied positions
- Reward tokens may be dynamically added or removed from the reward list
- Reward claiming involves external calls to third-party reward contracts (Convex, Aura, Curve gauges)
- Reward accounting uses a per-share accumulator pattern (rewardPerToken)

hints: Search for swap calls where `amountOutMin` or `minOut` is set to 0 or not provided
Check if slippage tolerance is hardcoded rather than user-configurable
Look for UniswapV3 `swap()` calls that rely on `sqrtPriceLimitX96` for slippage protection -- this causes partial fills, not reverts
Check if swap deadlines use `block.timestamp` (always passes) instead of a user-supplied future timestamp
Verify that reward token swaps during position close/harvest have slippage protection
Look for multi-hop swaps where only the final output is checked but intermediate steps can be exploited
preconditions:
- Protocol performs token swaps (DEX interactions) as part of its core operations (opening/closing positions, liquidation, yield distribution)
- Slippage parameters are either hardcoded, incorrectly applied, or missing entirely
- Users cannot control the minimum output amount for swaps performed on their behalf
- Swap transactions may be pending in the mempool, susceptible to sandwich attacks or stale execution
